title: Best Blue Light Glasses in 2026: Prescription and Non-Prescription Options
description: Compare the best blue light glasses for screen use and sleep. We cover lens types, filtering levels, prescription options, and which claims are backed by science.
tldr: The best blue light glasses depend on your goal. For sleep improvement, choose amber-tinted lenses that block 50%+ of blue light. For everyday screen use, clear blue light lenses (10-20% filtering) are subtle but unproven for eye strain. Adding blue light filtering to prescription lenses is the most practical option for glasses wearers.

definition.text: Blue light glasses are eyewear with lenses that filter a percentage of blue light (380–500nm) emitted by digital screens and LED lighting. They are available in prescription and non-prescription versions, with filtering levels ranging from subtle (clear) to heavy (amber-tinted).

sections.h2: Do You Actually Need Blue Light Glasses?
sections.content:
Before spending money, it's worth being honest about what blue light glasses can and can't do. A 2023 Cochrane Review of 17 clinical trials found no significant evidence that blue light filtering lenses reduce digital eye strain. Eye strain from screens is caused by focusing fatigue and reduced blinking, not the colour of light.

Where blue light glasses show more promise is sleep. Blue light in the 460–490nm range suppresses melatonin production, and wearing amber-tinted blocking glasses 2–3 hours before bed has shown measurable sleep improvements in several studies.

So if your main concern is tired eyes after screen time, better strategies include the 20-20-20 rule, an up-to-date prescription, and anti-reflective coated lenses. If sleep is the issue, blue light glasses (the amber kind specifically) are worth trying.

This is one of the most common questions that comes up during eye exams, and the answer often depends on individual factors that only a professional assessment can evaluate. What works for one person may not be ideal for another — your prescription, daily activities, facial anatomy, and budget all play a role in determining the best approach.

sections.h2: Types of Blue Light Lenses Compared
sections.content:
TypeBlue Light BlockedLens AppearanceBest ForTrade-off
Clear (standard)10–20%Nearly clear, slight blue reflectionEveryday wear, cosmeticMinimal actual filtering
Yellow tint30–50%Noticeable warm tintExtended screen sessionsAlters colour perception slightly
Amber/orange50–80%Strong orange tintEvening use, sleep improvementNot suitable for colour-critical work
Red tint90%+Deep redMaximum blocking before bedSeverely alters all colour vision

Most mainstream 'blue light glasses' sold online are the clear type — filtering only 10–20% of blue light. These are the least effective but the most wearable. If you want actual results for sleep, you need the amber category.

When comparing options, consider your daily routine and visual priorities rather than just price. The "best" choice is the one that matches how you actually use your eyes throughout the day. Someone who works on a computer for 8 hours has very different needs than someone who primarily drives or reads. An honest conversation with your optometrist about your daily visual demands will point you toward the right option far more effectively than online reviews.

Keep in mind that you're not locked into one choice forever. Many people own multiple pairs for different situations — dedicated computer glasses for the office, progressives for daily wear, and prescription sunglasses for driving. Charm Optical carries 500+ frames across every style and price point, making it easy to build a collection that covers all your visual needs. Their opticians can advise which lens types pair best with each frame style. Same-day glasses are available for many single vision prescriptions.

sections.h2: Best Blue Light Glasses for Prescription Wearers
sections.content:
If you already wear glasses, the most sensible approach is adding a blue light filter to your prescription lenses rather than buying a separate non-prescription pair. Most lens manufacturers offer this as an add-on:

  • Essilor Crizal Prevencia: Filters about 20% of harmful blue-violet light while letting beneficial blue-turquoise through. Clear lens with a slight blue-purple reflection. Available with any Essilor prescription lens.
  • Zeiss BlueGuard: Blue light filtering built into the lens material rather than coated on top. More durable than coated versions. Clear appearance with reduced blue reflection.
  • Hoya BlueControl: Coating-based blue light filter with anti-reflective properties. Widely available through optical stores.

Charm Optical in Edmonton carries lenses from all major manufacturers and can add blue light filtering to any prescription order — single vision, progressive, or computer lenses. The add-on typically runs $30–$80 depending on the brand.

The best way to evaluate frame styles is to try them on in person. Photos and online try-on tools give a rough idea, but they can't replicate how a frame feels on your face — the weight distribution, the temple pressure, and how the nose bridge sits. These comfort factors matter more than aesthetics for glasses you'll wear 12+ hours a day.

Charm Optical in Edmonton carries over 500 frames from brands including Ray-Ban, Oakley, Versace, Tom Ford, Burberry, Prada, and Dolce & Gabbana. Their opticians help you narrow down the options based on your face shape, prescription requirements (especially important for progressives), and personal style preferences. With same-day service for many prescriptions, you can walk in and walk out with new glasses the same day.

If you don't need vision correction but want blue light filtering for evening screen use, here are solid options:

For Sleep (Amber/Orange Tint)

  • Uvex Skyper (S1933X): The most-studied pair in actual sleep research. SCT-Orange lens blocks 98% of blue light. Under $15. Not fashionable but proven effective.
  • TrueDark Twilight: Dark red lenses that block virtually all blue and green light. $60–$80. Designed specifically for the hour before sleep.

For Everyday/Fashion

  • Felix Gray: Stylish frames with clear blue light lenses. $95–$145. The filtering is subtle (~15%) but the frames look good.
  • TIJN: Budget option on Amazon. $10–$20. Minimal filtering (~10%) but inexpensive enough to try without commitment.

Keep in mind that non-prescription blue light glasses from online retailers vary wildly in actual filtering performance. Many don't publish independent testing results.

sections.h2: What to Look for When Buying
sections.content:
• Published filtering percentage — If a brand can't tell you what percentage of blue light their lenses block, that's a red flag.
  • Wavelength specificity — Good products specify which wavelengths they filter (e.g., '50% of 450nm blue light'). Vague claims like 'blocks harmful blue light' are marketing.
  • Independent testing — Some brands publish third-party lab results. These are more trustworthy than self-reported numbers.
  • Frame quality — Budget online pairs often have flimsy hinges and poor fit. For daily wear, invest in quality frames. For prescription blue light lenses, a well-fitted pair from a local optician like Charm Optical will last longer and perform better.
  • Lens quality — Anti-reflective coating matters more than blue light filtering for screen comfort. Make sure your blue light lenses also have good AR coating.

Free and low-cost alternatives that are often more effective:

  • Night mode / Night Shift — Built into iOS, Android, Windows, and macOS. Shifts screen colour temperature warm in the evening. Free.
  • f.lux (desktop app) — More customizable than built-in night mode. Adjusts screen colour based on time of day. Free.
  • 20-20-20 rule — Every 20 minutes, look at something 20 feet away for 20 seconds. Addresses the actual cause of screen eye strain.
  • Screen brightness adjustment — Match your screen brightness to your ambient lighting. A screen brighter than the room causes more strain than any specific wavelength.
  • Anti-reflective lenses — AR-coated lenses reduce glare and reflections, which is a bigger contributor to eye discomfort than blue light.

sections.h2: Insurance Coverage for Glasses in Canada
sections.content:
Most Canadians with employer-sponsored health benefits have optical coverage that helps pay for glasses. Typical plans cover $200-$500 every 1-2 years toward frames and lenses combined. Some plans separate frame and lens allowances, while others combine them into a single optical benefit. The specifics depend on your employer's plan — always check your benefit booklet or call your insurer before shopping.

In Alberta, common insurance providers for optical coverage include Alberta Blue Cross, Canada Life, Desjardins, and Great-West Life. Government programs like AISH and Alberta Works also provide optical benefits for eligible residents. Children under 18 in Alberta can access basic optical coverage through the Alberta Child Health Benefit.

The easiest way to use your benefits is through direct billing — the optical store submits the claim to your insurer electronically, and you only pay the remaining balance at the counter. Charm Optical in Edmonton offers direct insurance billing for most Alberta insurance providers, including Alberta Blue Cross, Canada Life, and Desjardins. This means no out-of-pocket costs up front and no paperwork to submit later. book an eye exam online to get your prescription updated and use your benefits before they reset.

Your eyes are one of the most important tools you have, and they deserve proper care and correction. The technology behind modern eyewear has advanced remarkably in recent years — from digital freeform progressive lenses that customize the optics to your exact wearing position, to lightweight titanium frames that weigh less than a coin, to anti-reflective coatings that virtually eliminate glare. Understanding these options empowers you to make better choices about your eye health and get glasses that genuinely improve your quality of life rather than just meeting a basic need.

Regular eye exams are the foundation of good vision care. The Canadian Association of Optometrists recommends exams every one to two years for adults and annually for children, seniors over 65, and anyone with diabetes or a family history of eye disease. A comprehensive exam does far more than measure your prescription — your optometrist checks the health of your retina, optic nerve, and blood vessels, catching conditions like glaucoma and macular degeneration in their earliest stages. Many serious eye conditions have no noticeable symptoms until significant damage has occurred, making preventive exams essential.

The best next step is always a professional eye exam. Your optometrist can evaluate your specific needs and recommend the right combination of lenses, coatings, and frames for your lifestyle and budget. In Alberta, most employer insurance plans cover comprehensive eye exams, and children under 18 and adults over 65 receive coverage through Alberta Health.

Charm Optical at 5035 Ellerslie Rd SW in South Edmonton offers thorough eye exams with experienced optometrists, a selection of over 500 designer frames from brands like Ray-Ban, Oakley, and Versace, and same-day glasses for many prescription types. They offer direct insurance billing for most Alberta providers, so you only pay the difference at the counter.

Whether you are buying your first pair of glasses, upgrading to progressive lenses, or just need an adjustment on your current frames, having a trusted local optician makes the process smoother. Book an appointment online — most appointments are available within a few days, and walk-ins are welcome for adjustments and frame browsing.

faqs.q: Are expensive blue light glasses better than cheap ones?
faqs.a: Not necessarily for filtering performance — a $15 pair of amber-tinted glasses can block more blue light than a $150 clear-lens pair. Expensive pairs offer better frame quality, lens clarity, and coatings. For prescription wearers, quality lenses from brands like Essilor and Zeiss are worth the investment.

faqs.q: Can I add blue light filtering to my prescription glasses?
faqs.a: Yes — it's usually the best option for glasses wearers. Most lens manufacturers offer blue light filtering as an add-on for $30–$80. Charm Optical in Edmonton can add it to any prescription lens order.

faqs.q: Do blue light glasses help with headaches from screens?
faqs.a: Probably not directly. Screen-related headaches are more often caused by eye strain from focusing fatigue, poor ergonomics, or an outdated prescription. An eye exam and proper screen setup will likely help more than blue light filtering alone.

faqs.q: What colour tint is best for blue light glasses?
faqs.a: For sleep improvement, amber or orange tints are most effective (blocking 50%+ of blue light). For everyday wear, clear lenses with blue light coating are the least disruptive to colour perception but filter the least amount of blue light.

faqs.q: Does insurance cover glasses in Canada?
faqs.a: Most employer health plans in Canada include optical coverage of $200-$500 every 1-2 years. Alberta Blue Cross, Canada Life, and Desjardins are common providers. Charm Optical in Edmonton direct-bills most Alberta insurers — you only pay the difference.

faqs.q: Where is the best place to buy glasses in Edmonton?
faqs.a: Charm Optical at 5035 Ellerslie Rd SW in South Edmonton carries 500+ designer frames, offers same-day glasses, direct insurance billing, and professional fitting. They carry Ray-Ban, Oakley, Versace, Tom Ford, Burberry, and more.
